followUpsQA.a: Memoization caches the result for a given input. It only works correctly on pure functions - because the output is entirely determined by the input, the cached value will always be correct.

dialogue.text: What is a pure function?

A pure function has two rules: it is deterministic - same inputs always produce the same output - and it has no side effects - it does not modify anything outside itself, like external variables, the DOM, or its arguments.
What counts as a side effect?

Mutating external state, making API calls, logging to the console, reading the system clock, or modifying the function's arguments. Anything observable outside the return value is a side effect. Even `console.log` is technically impure because it changes the console output - though in practice people ignore that one.
Why do Redux reducers need to be pure?

React StrictMode calls reducers twice deliberately to surface side effects. If the reducer mutates state, the second call produces a different result and the UI state becomes inconsistent. Pure reducers return a new state object instead of mutating, so calling twice gives the same result both times.
What practical benefit do pure functions give you?

Testability and memoization. Testing a pure function is just calling it with input and asserting the output - no mocks, no beforeEach setup, no teardown. Memoization also only works on pure functions: `memoize(calculateShipping)` caches results by input. If the function read external state, the cache would return stale results.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What causes memory leaks in JavaScript and how do you avoid them?

answer.oneLiner: Memory leaks happen when references are kept alive unintentionally, preventing the garbage collector from freeing memory. Common causes are forgotten timers, un-removed event listeners, and closures holding large objects.
answer.detail: JavaScript uses garbage collection - it automatically frees memory when no references to an object remain. A memory leak is when you accidentally keep a reference alive longer than needed. The most common causes: timers (`setInterval`) that are never cleared keep running and hold references to their callbacks' closures. Event listeners added in component setup but never removed after the component is destroyed hold references to the component. Detached DOM nodes - elements removed from the DOM but still referenced by JavaScript variables - cannot be collected. Closures that capture large objects keep those objects alive for the closure's lifetime. Accidental global variables (without `var`/`let`/`const` in sloppy mode) persist forever. Large caches that grow unboundedly. `WeakMap` and `WeakRef` allow holding references without preventing garbage collection.
keyPoints: Forgotten `setInterval` and `setTimeout` callbacks keep their closures alive - always `clearInterval`
Event listeners not removed after component teardown - always `removeEventListener` in cleanup
Detached DOM nodes still referenced in JavaScript variables cannot be garbage-collected
Use `WeakMap`/`WeakSet` for caches keyed by objects - entries are collected when the key object is gone

code.note: Always return or expose a cleanup function for timers and listeners - React useEffect return value is exactly this pattern.

answer.analogy: A memory leak is like a hotel room where the key is lost but the room is permanently booked - it can never be given to another guest.
redFlags: Not knowing that `setInterval` without `clearInterval` causes a memory leak
Not removing event listeners on component teardown
Storing DOM element references in module-level variables after those elements are removed from the DOM
Not knowing WeakMap exists as an alternative for object-keyed caches

followUpsQA.q: How would you diagnose a memory leak in Chrome DevTools?
followUpsQA.a: Take a heap snapshot in the Memory panel, perform the action you suspect leaks, take another snapshot, then compare. Look for growing retained objects and detached DOM nodes.

followUpsQA.q: When would you use WeakMap over Map?
followUpsQA.a: When you want to attach extra data to objects without preventing their garbage collection. A WeakMap key being collected automatically removes the entry, so the cache never grows indefinitely.

followUpsQA.q: What is a detached DOM node leak?
followUpsQA.a: A DOM element removed from the document but still referenced by a JavaScript variable. The garbage collector cannot collect it because there is still a reference, even though it is invisible on the page.

followUpsQA.q: How does React prevent listener leak patterns?
followUpsQA.a: The `useEffect` cleanup function - the function returned from `useEffect` is called before the component unmounts. This is where you call `clearInterval`, `removeEventListener`, or unsubscribe from observables.

dialogue.text: What causes memory leaks in JavaScript and how do you prevent them?

Leaks happen when you unintentionally keep references alive, preventing the garbage collector from freeing memory. The three most common causes I encounter: uncleaned setInterval timers, event listeners not removed on teardown, and detached DOM nodes still held in variables.
Walk me through the setInterval leak specifically.

When you call `setInterval(callback, 5000)`, the browser holds a reference to the callback forever until you call `clearInterval`. The callback is typically a closure over component state or DOM elements. Even after the component unmounts and is removed from the page, those objects stay in memory because the interval holds a reference chain to them.
How do you handle this in a React component?

In `useEffect`, return a cleanup function: `return () => clearInterval(id)`. React calls this cleanup before the component unmounts. Same for event listeners - `window.addEventListener` in useEffect, `window.removeEventListener` in the cleanup. This is the standard pattern and prevents the leak.
What is WeakMap and when would you reach for it?

WeakMap stores key-value pairs where the keys must be objects, and the keys are held weakly - they do not prevent garbage collection. If the key object is collected, the WeakMap entry is automatically removed. Useful for caching computed results per object without causing the object to live longer than needed, like caching a serialized representation per model instance.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: Predict the output: var a=20; Abc(); function Abc(){ var a=10; console.log(a); }

answer.oneLiner: It logs `10`. The function declaration is hoisted so calling it before its line works. Inside, `var a=10` creates a separate function-scoped variable that shadows the outer `a=20`.
answer.detail: Two JavaScript concepts work together here. First, hoisting: function declarations are fully hoisted, so `Abc()` can be called before its line in the file. Second, function scope: `var a = 10` inside `Abc` creates a completely separate variable from the outer `var a = 20`. These two variables are in different scopes and have no connection. The inner `a` shadows the outer `a` within the function, so `console.log(a)` inside `Abc` sees `10`. The outer `a = 20` is completely unaffected and still holds `20` after the function call.
keyPoints: Function declarations are fully hoisted - `Abc()` can be called before its definition
Inner `var a = 10` is function-scoped to `Abc` - completely separate from the outer `a`
The inner `a` shadows the outer `a` within `Abc`'s scope
The outer `a = 20` is unchanged after the function call

code.note: Output is 10 - hoisting lets Abc() run before its definition, and the inner var a shadows the outer a.

answer.analogy: The inner variable is a local copy with the same name - changing or reading it does not touch the original.
redFlags: Saying the output is 20 - the inner var a creates a separate function-scoped variable
Saying the call fails because Abc is not defined yet - function declarations are hoisted
Thinking the inner assignment modifies the outer a - they are in separate scopes
Confusing var function scope with let block scope - var ignores inner blocks but creates separate scope per function

followUpsQA.q: What is the output?
followUpsQA.a: `10` - the inner `var a` creates a new function-scoped variable that shadows the outer one.

followUpsQA.q: Why can `Abc` be called before its definition?
followUpsQA.a: Because function declarations are fully hoisted - both the name and body are available before execution reaches that line.

followUpsQA.q: What is the value of the outer `a` after calling `Abc()`?
followUpsQA.a: Still `20` - the inner `var a = 10` is a completely separate variable in `Abc`'s scope and does not affect the outer one.

followUpsQA.q: What is variable shadowing?
followUpsQA.a: When an inner scope declares a variable with the same name as an outer one. The inner variable takes precedence within that scope, making the outer one inaccessible by that name.

dialogue.text: What does this snippet log and why? `var a=20; Abc(); function Abc(){ var a=10; console.log(a); }`

It logs `10`. Two things happen. First, function declarations are fully hoisted, so calling `Abc()` before its definition in the file works fine. Second, `var a = 10` inside `Abc` creates a completely new function-scoped variable - it shadows the outer `a = 20` but is not the same variable.
What is the value of the outer `a` after the call?
Still `20`. The inner `var a` in `Abc` is scoped to that function. Nothing the function does affects the outer `a`. They share a name but are completely independent variables in separate scopes.
What if `Abc` had not used `var` for its `a`?

Then `a = 10` would look up the scope chain and find the outer `a`, modifying it to `10`. The outer `a` would be `10` after the call, and `console.log(a)` would still print `10` - but now the outer variable would have been changed as a side effect.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: Predict the output: var a=[1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9,0]; a[40]=40; console.log(a.length);

answer.oneLiner: It logs `41`. When you assign to index 40, JavaScript creates a sparse array with holes at indices 10-39, and `length` is always the highest index plus one.
answer.detail: JavaScript arrays are not fixed-size containers - they are objects with numeric keys. The `length` property is not a count of elements; it is always `highest_index + 1`. When you assign `a[40] = 40` on an array with 10 elements (indices 0-9), JavaScript extends the array to index 40 without filling the gap. Indices 10-39 become 'holes' - they exist conceptually but hold `undefined` when accessed and are skipped by `forEach`, `map`, and `filter`. This is called a sparse array. `a.length` becomes 41 (index 40 + 1). The array has 11 actual values (the original 10 plus the one at index 40) but a length of 41.
keyPoints: `length` is always the highest assigned index plus 1 - not the count of elements
Assigning beyond the current length creates a sparse array with empty holes
Holes read as `undefined` but are skipped by array iteration methods like `forEach` and `map`
The array has 11 actual values but a length of 41

code.note: Sparse arrays have length equal to max index + 1, not element count - always filter if you need only real values.

answer.analogy: Length is like a house number - if you build house 40 on an empty street, the street extends to 40 even though houses 10-39 do not exist yet.
redFlags: Saying the output is 11 (the number of actual values) - length is max index + 1
Not knowing what a sparse array is or that holes exist
Thinking holes throw an error when accessed - they silently return undefined
Not knowing that forEach and map skip holes while for...of does not

followUpsQA.q: What is the output?
followUpsQA.a: `41` - assigning to index 40 makes 40 the highest index, so length becomes 40 + 1 = 41.

followUpsQA.q: What is a sparse array?
followUpsQA.a: An array with gaps - some indices between 0 and length-1 have no assigned value. Accessing them returns `undefined`, and iteration methods like `forEach` skip them.

followUpsQA.q: How is `length` computed?
followUpsQA.a: It is always the highest assigned index plus 1, not the count of real elements.

followUpsQA.q: What does `a[15]` return in this example?
followUpsQA.a: `undefined` - index 15 is a hole. No value was assigned there, so it returns undefined without throwing.

followUpsQA.q: How do forEach and for...of treat holes differently?
followUpsQA.a: `forEach` and `map` skip holes entirely. `for...of` iterates the full range and includes holes as `undefined` values.

dialogue.text: What does `console.log(a.length)` print after `a[40] = 40` on a 10-element array?

It prints `41`. The `length` property of a JavaScript array is not a count of actual elements - it is always the highest assigned index plus one. Assigning to index 40 makes 40 the highest index, so length becomes 41.
What happened to indices 10 through 39?

They became holes - the array is now sparse. JavaScript did not fill those slots. If you access `a[15]`, you get `undefined`. No error, no crash - just a silent undefined. The array has 11 real values but a length of 41.
How do iteration methods treat those holes?

`forEach`, `map`, and `filter` skip holes entirely - they only visit indices that actually have a value. `for...of` iterates the full range from 0 to length-1 and returns `undefined` for each hole. This difference matters when processing data that might have gaps.
When would a sparse array appear in a real app?

Usually by accident - assigning to an index far beyond the current end, or by calling `new Array(100)` which creates a 100-slot sparse array with no elements. In an invoicing system someone might use invoice numbers as array indices, creating a huge sparse array. Using a plain object or Map as a key-value store would be the right fix.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅
